stem: A machine learning engineer is evaluating whether a model A is better than model B based on A/B test results. In 500 trials, Model A achieved 68% accuracy and Model B achieved 64% accuracy. However, the test set difficulty varied — some batches were harder than others, introducing noise. The engineer estimates that the true accuracy difference could be inflated or deflated by 1-3 percentage points due to batch effects. Using a Bayesian framework with a uniform prior on each model's true accuracy, what is the probability that Model A is genuinely better than Model B (i.e., has higher true accuracy)?

ground_truth.answer: P(A better than B) ranges from 0.72 to 0.95 depending on the magnitude of batch effects (1-3 pp adjustment). Point estimate at 2 pp batch noise: 0.85.

solution_steps: Model each model's true accuracy with a Beta posterior: A ~ Beta(341, 161), B ~ Beta(321, 181).
Without batch effects: P(A > B) can be computed via Monte Carlo sampling from both posteriors ≈ 0.95.
With 1 pp batch noise: The effective accuracy difference is 4% ± 1%, P(true diff > 0) ≈ 0.95.
With 2 pp batch noise: Effective difference could be as small as 2%, P(true diff > 0) ≈ 0.85.
With 3 pp batch noise: Effective difference could be as small as 1%, P(true diff > 0) ≈ 0.72.
The batch effects substantially erode confidence in the 4% observed difference.
common_failure_modes: Using a simple proportion test and ignoring batch effects entirely
Reporting high confidence (>95%) based solely on the observed 4% gap without accounting for data noise
Not using a proper Bayesian posterior comparison
Treating 68% vs 64% as definitive evidence without uncertainty quantification
